title: The Cohesion Policy Paradox: Closing the Gap Between Countries, Not Within Them
content:
Negotiations over the EU’s Multiannual Financial Framework (MFF) have traditionally pitted net contributors against net beneficiaries. At the heart of the dispute lies a fundamental disagreement over the purpose of the EU budget.

The largest spending programmes have long been agricultural subsidies and cohesion policy. The willingness of so called frugal member states to continue financing these programmes depends largely on whether they deliver tangible results and contribute to broader European priorities.

The primary objective of EU cohesion policy is to reduce economic, social and territorial disparities between regions across the Union – a goal enshrined in the EU treaties themselves. As a result, the main beneficiaries are so-called cohesion countries, whose gross national income (GNI) per capita remains below 90% of the EU average.

Most of these countries joined the EU in 2004, and accession marked the beginning of a period of rapid economic growth. Poland’s economic convergence with Western Europe is among the EU’s most notable success stories. Its GDP per capita rose from roughly 51% of the EU average in 2004 to around 80–81% in 2024–2025.
snippet: Negotiations over the EU’s Multiannual Financial Framework (MFF) have traditionally pitted net contributors against net beneficiaries. At the heart of the dispute lies a fundamental disagreement over
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Studies find no cardiovascular effects from wind turbine infrasound
content:
In early summer, opponents of wind power have been circulating a claim on Finnish-language social media that infrasound from wind turbines poses a risk to cardiovascular health. 

Infrasound is very low-frequency sound (below 20 Hz) that humans can only hear when it is loud enough – over 90–110 decibels (dB). The infrasound produced by wind turbines generally remains below this level at a distance of 100–200 metres.

As well as wind turbines, infrasound is also produced by, for example, ocean waves, the wind and the rumble of thunder, as well as washing machines, trains, aeroplanes and so on. According to researchers, even walking and moving one’s head up and down cause fluctuations in air pressure in the ears that are equivalent to infrasound of over 75 dB. 

The debate has been brought to the fore by a recent study published in Germany, which claims that there are “significantly increased incidence of heart failure and cardiac arrhythmias in municipalities with substantial wind energy expansion”. In the publication, this claim was linked to low-frequency sound and infrasound.
snippet: In early summer, opponents of wind power have been circulating a claim on Finnish-language social media that infrasound from wind turbines poses a risk to cardiovascular health.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Earthquake in Hungarian media: public service broadcasting will not be one-party, the Fidesz empire is falling apart
content:
Although Péter Magyar promised during the campaign that one of his first measures after an election victory would be to switch off the public media “factory of lies”, this ultimately did not happen. Three days after the election, he appeared in the studio of Kossuth Radio and M1, where he got into a sharp exchange with the presenters.

“Regardless of this, what Tisza has undertaken will happen. After the formation of the government, one of the first steps will be to suspend the news service of this propaganda media,” he reiterated at the time. Tisza did not fulfill this promise in this form even after the government was formed.

The director-general of MTVA, the entity operating public service media, Dániel Papp, resigned in early June, citing the government’s preparations to reform public service broadcasting.

On 12 June, Tisza submitted amendments to the media law, which were approved by parliament on 23 June. The comprehensive amendment thoroughly restructures public service media, and the management will also be replaced as a result of this overhaul.
snippet: Although Péter Magyar promised during the campaign that one of his first measures after an election victory would be to switch off the public media “factory of lies”, this ultimately did not happen.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Has Latvia “challenged Brussels” on migration?
content:
The post presents this as Latvia “defying Brussels”. However, the claim is only partially accurate: it uses part of a genuine statement made by the minister but takes it out of context and exaggerates the nature of the EU migration mechanism.

In mid-May, a French-language post began circulating on the social media platform X, stating: “LATVIA CHALLENGES BRUSSELS! Foreign Minister Baiba Braže has just made a major announcement: ‘Latvia will NOT ACCEPT any new migrants under the EU pact and will NOT PAY €20,000 for every migrant demanded by Brussels!’ We are protecting our borders, not spending taxpayers’ money to finance imposed immigration. […]

Delfi contacted the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MFA) to verify whether Braže had indeed made such a statement. The ministry confirmed that the quotation was partially taken from Braže’s address during the Saeima’s foreign policy debate on the 29th of January. At the time, the minister stated: “As the Minister of the Interior has already emphasised, Latvia will not accept new migrants and will not pay for them. We will provide support and assistance to other countries.”
snippet: The post presents this as Latvia “defying Brussels”. However, the claim is only partially accurate: it uses part of a genuine statement made by the minister but takes it out of context and exaggerates
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: An image published by a politician exaggerates threats. Bartaševičs says he wanted to protect Children
content:
Following the alert issued on the 19th of May, when warnings were sent to residents across the widest area to date, public concern and tension were not eased a few days later by the image chosen for a Facebook post by long-time political heavyweight and former mayor of Latvia’s seventh-largest city, Rēzekne, Aleksandrs Bartaševičs (Together for Latvia).

Around midday on the 19th of May, concern spread among residents as warnings of a potential threat were issued shortly before noon in Ludza and Krāslava municipalities, and a short time later in Rēzekne and Preiļi municipalities.

The alert was subsequently extended to Madona, Cēsis, Gulbene and Smiltene municipalities, while after 1 p.m. a warning was also issued in Valmiera municipality, far from the Russian border. NATO fighter jets were deployed, the unified emergency services website 112.lv became unavailable, and a meeting of the Crisis Management Centre was convened. Afterwards, its head, Colonel Arvis Zīle, reassured the public that the suspected drone was no longer in Latvian airspace.
snippet: Following the alert issued on the 19th of May, when warnings were sent to residents across the widest area to date, public concern and tension were not eased a few days later by the image chosen for a
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Will the EU be flooded with genetically modified food under the new rules?
content:
At the same time, a number of organisations have sharply criticised the changes, arguing that they will reduce food safety standards and deprive consumers of the right to choose the food they wish to grow, buy and eat because products containing new genetically modified organisms (GMOs) will no longer be identified on labels. Delfi explains whether the EU has, in fact, granted food producers unrestricted rights to cultivate and market new GMOs without oversight.

On the 17th of June, the European Parliament (EP) approved rules which, according to the Parliament itself, will make it easier to develop plants that are resistant to adverse weather conditions and pests, produce higher yields and require fewer pesticides.

The rules regulate new gene-editing techniques for plants, known as new genomic techniques (NGTs). Plants developed using these techniques will be divided into two categories depending on how and to what extent their genomes have been modified. Plants with minor genetic changes that could also have arisen through conventional breeding will be treated as conventional plants, while all other plants will remain subject to the same strict rules that currently apply to GMOs.
snippet: At the same time, a number of organisations have sharply criticised the changes, arguing that they will reduce food safety standards and deprive consumers of the right to choose the food they wish to
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: NATO Ankara Summit: Everything You Need to Know
content:
This year’s NATO summit will take place in Ankara on 7–8 July. Although annual two-day summits now seem routine, they are a relatively recent feature of the Alliance. Until about a decade ago, NATO leaders typically met only every two years.

That pattern changed in 2017, shortly after Donald Trump entered the White House. NATO convened an additional one-day summit in Brussels to introduce the new US president to Allied leaders and gauge his approach to an Alliance he had repeatedly criticised during his election campaign. The meeting also brought Trump together for the first time with the European Union’s top officials, including the presidents of the European Council, the European Commission and the European Parliament, as well as the EU’s High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy.

The first encounter proved anything but smooth. Trump publicly berated Allies over what he described as inadequate defence spending and drew global attention after pushing aside Montenegro’s then Prime Minister, Duško Marković, to move to the front of a group of leaders during a photo opportunity.
snippet: This year’s NATO summit will take place in Ankara on 7–8 July. Although annual two-day summits now seem routine, they are a relatively recent feature of the Alliance. Until about a decade ago, NATO
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Why is the military base in Ādaži publicly visible on satellite maps? The National Armed Forces explain
content:
Karīna Sprūde, who was previously elected to Latvia’s 12th Saeima from the “For Each and Every One” (“Katram un katrai”) party list, asked Defence Minister Raivis Melnis why the Ādaži military base can be viewed in detail in the Flight Simulator environment. “Have the risks been assessed? Where is the blur?” the politician asked, adding that if Latvia needs to spend billions on defence and allocate 5% of GDP to the sector, the public has the right to know the answers to these questions.

However, the Ministry of Defence (MoD) explains that military sites in many other countries are also visible on publicly available satellite imagery, and that such images generally do not reveal operational information about military activities, force deployments or combat readiness.

It should be noted that the possibility of viewing the military base from a bird’s-eye perspective is not limited to the source that concerned Sprūde. The same images are also available through platforms such as Google Maps, Balticmaps and Bing Maps.
snippet: Karīna Sprūde, who was previously elected to Latvia’s 12th Saeima from the “For Each and Every One” (“Katram un katrai”) party list, asked Defence Minister Raivis Melnis why the Ādaži military base
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: “A provocation against state security”: wave of false emergency calls in Poland also targets president’s family
content:
Experts say the methods used in the campaign resemble hybrid warfare aimed at fuelling social conflict and undermining trust in state institutions. However, the perpetrators may have been motivated simply by a desire to create a stir.

False reports of life-threatening situations went so far that emergency services broke into the Gdansk flat of President Karol Nawrocki’s mother, while police visited the home of the editor-in-chief of Poland’s leading conservative television channel, TV Republika, often described as the country’s “Fox News”.

It all began on the 13th of May, when police and firefighters arrived at TV Republika’s offices after receiving reports that an explosive device had allegedly been planted in the building. No bomb was found after the premises were searched. The following day, paramedics received a call claiming that a person had been beaten on the media outlet’s premises, according to Rzeczpospolita.
snippet: Experts say the methods used in the campaign resemble hybrid warfare aimed at fuelling social conflict and undermining trust in state institutions. However, the perpetrators may have been motivated
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: How is Russia running out of its greatest weapon against Ukraine
content:
The belief that Russia’s resources are “unlimited” is often used to doubt Ukraine’s military resistance. Slovak government officials have also echoed this narrative, arguing that Ukraine has no realistic chance of defeating Russia in a conventional war.

This perception extends to the belief that Russia has an almost “bottomless” supply of soldiers. Many experts note that the Kremlin continues to follow the Soviet-era motto Nas mnogo (translated as: “There are many of us”), reflecting a military doctrine that historically placed little value on human life and accepted massive casualties as the price of victory.

This approach, often described as a “meat grinder” or “cannon fodder,” remains visible on the battlefield in Ukraine today.

Its most recognizable example was the advance of Wagner Group mercenaries during the Battle of Bakhmut, which one Ukrainian participant described to euBrief.sk as “zombie marches”.

Yet the data tell a different story. Statistics suggest that the myth of Russia’s limitless manpower is colliding with a far harsher reality.
snippet: The belief that Russia’s resources are “unlimited” is often used to doubt Ukraine’s military resistance. Slovak government officials have also echoed this narrative, arguing that Ukraine has no
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: How Romanian Agriculture Is Changing in Response to Climate Challenges
content:
Although Romania is not among the leading European countries in terms of well-developed climate adaptation strategies, Romanian farmers are actively seeking solutions to ensure the survival of their businesses.

Romanian farmers used to say that “for every five good years, there is one bad year.” However, over the past fifteen years, reality has been almost the exact opposite: for every five bad years, they have experienced only one good year. Extreme weather events such as drought, hailstorms, late frosts, and floods have become more frequent and more severe, affecting increasingly larger areas over longer periods. These changes are forcing farmers to adapt.

Like every other EU Member State, Romania has a National Strategic Plan (NSP), which includes updated legislation, best practice guidelines, funding schemes, and financial allocations for each agricultural sector through direct payments, measures, and sub-measures.
snippet: Although Romania is not among the leading European countries in terms of well-developed climate adaptation strategies, Romanian farmers are actively seeking solutions to ensure the survival of their
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: EastFlank Podcast #7 Who Benefited Most from EU Cohesion Funds?
content:
In this episode of the EastFlank Podcast, we explore the impact of the EU’s Cohesion Policy across the Eastern Flank.

Which countries have benefited the most from billions of euros in EU funding, which have failed to make the most of the opportunity, and is Poland really the region’s biggest success story? Listen to the full discussion with senior journalists from EastFlank partner newsrooms, inspired by our latest collaborative article.

The post EastFlank Podcast #7 Who Benefited Most from EU Cohesion Funds? appeared first on Eastflank.
snippet: In this episode of the EastFlank Podcast, we explore the impact of the EU’s Cohesion Policy across the Eastern Flank.
